narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our predictive tool saw such a high risk—97% chance—of you struggling to pass the bar exam, leading to this tough call on your application. Picture the model piecing together your story like chapters in a book: it started with your Year 1 grades landing you in the bottom decile at school, the single strongest signal flashing caution, as if that early stumble set a shaky foundation that made everything else harder to build on. Then came your Year 3 decile, also at the bottom, reinforcing that pattern of academic hurdles persisting over time, pulling even more weight toward the prediction of failure. Your LSAT score of 24 stood out next as a major concern, whispering that the raw analytical skills needed for bar success just weren't showing up strongly enough yet. Layered on top was your middle-family income bracket, which the model linked to added pressures that often tip the scales against passing in similar profiles. Your Asian background played a role too, as the tool noted patterns where it correlated with tougher odds in this context. Your undergrad GPA of 2.6 added to the stack, suggesting the college rigor might not fully prepare you for law's demands. Even being full-time and female entered the picture, each nudging the outlook downward based on historical trends the model has seen. It's this whole combination—your bottom deciles bookending your school performance, that LSAT, and the ripple of supporting factors—that painted a picture of high risk, not any one thing alone. This isn't about your potential or heart; it's the model protecting spots for those with smoother paths ahead. If you boost those early grades or LSAT in retakes, doors could reopen—keep pushing, you've got this spirit. We're rooting for your next chapter.
